lesson.menu_desc: Ability, permission and professional requests in online meetings -- 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- Can You Do This? Ability, Permission & Requests with Can/Could
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Carlos Eduardo | Aula 8 -- Can You Do This? (Can/Could)

vocab.title: Match each meeting verb to its meaning
defs: to ask for something in a polite way
to show your screen or a file to other people
to set a day and time for a meeting
to make something clearer, with fewer doubts
to give the reasons so people understand
to show and talk about your work to a group

paras: Every week, I have an online meeting with a colleague in Uganda. First, I open the call and I ask: 'Can you hear me clearly?' Then I share my screen. I can show the audit criteria and I can explain the main points. If the connection is bad, I ask: 'Could you repeat that, please?'

In the meeting, I make polite requests. I say: 'Can you send me the updated checklist?' or 'Could you confirm the date?' My colleague can share his documents too. At the end, we schedule the next call and I say goodbye.

We use can for ability and permission, and can or could for requests. Can is for me and for you: I can present, you can share. Could is more polite: 'Could you clarify this criterion?' After can and could, we use the base verb: 'Can you send it?', not 'Can you to send it'.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- A1 reading, can & could in meetings

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Carlos explains how to grow coffee on a farm.
Carlos uses can and could to do things and make polite requests in online meetings.
Carlos writes a long report about the EUDR regulation.

items: Carlos has an online meeting every week.
The text says: 'Every week, I have an online meeting with a colleague in Uganda.'
Carlos cannot share his screen in the meeting.
The text says: 'Then I share my screen. I can show the audit criteria.'
Could is more polite than can for a request.
The text says: 'Could is more polite: Could you clarify this criterion?'
After can we say 'Can you to send it'.
The text says we use the base verb: 'Can you send it?', not 'Can you to send it'.

parts: send the checklist today, but I

gapfill.text: Use can for ability and permission (I can present, Can I share?). Use can or could for requests; could is more polite. After can/could, use the base verb (send, not to send). Negative: can't = cannot.

bank.label: Useful meeting sentences with can and could
items: Can you send me the updated checklist?
I can explain the main criteria.
I can present the audit result today.
Could you clarify this point, please?

lf.title: Ability or Request? Choose the function

lf.text: can + base verb = ability (I can present). can / could + you = a request (Can you send...? Could you repeat...?). Could is the more polite choice.

items.situation: You want to show your screen. Ask for permission.
tips: Can I present my screen now?

items.situation: You did not understand. Ask politely to repeat.
tips: Sorry, could you say that again?

items.situation: You want the updated checklist. Make a request.
tips: Can you send me the updated checklist?
Could you send the checklist, please?

items.situation: Say one thing you can do in the meeting.
tips: I can present the audit result.

items: Open the meeting: greet, check the sound, and ask to share your screen. Use can.
Ask your colleague for a document: 'Can you send me...?' or 'Could you send me...?'
Ask your colleague to repeat and to clarify, using could.
The audio is bad. Say you can't hear well and ask to repeat, all in one turn.

answerkey.title: Reveal the meeting phrases
list: Open + sound check: 'Can you hear me clearly? Can I share my screen?'
Present + explain: 'I can present the criteria. I can explain the main points.'
Polite requests: 'Could you repeat that, please? Could you send me the checklist?'
answerkey.note: can = ability / permission. can or could = request; could is more polite. Base verb after can/could.

listenings.text: Let me tell you what I can do in an online meeting. I can open the call and check the sound. I can share my screen and I can present the audit criteria. I can explain the main points in simple English. When the connection is bad, I can ask my colleague to repeat. I can also send documents and schedule the next call. There is one thing I can't do well yet: I can't translate every technical term alone. But I can ask for help, and that is enough. That is my job in the meeting.

Hi Carlos, this is Emma with a quick note before our call. Could you share your screen at the start? I want to see the audit criteria. Could you also explain the two critical points? If I speak too fast, please ask me to repeat. Can you send me the updated checklist after the meeting? And could you schedule the next call for Friday morning? Thank you, Carlos. You can do this. See you soon.

extra_audio.text: First, Carlos opens the online meeting. He asks: Can you hear me clearly? Then he shares his screen. He can present the audit criteria. He asks his colleague: Could you send me the checklist? At the end, they schedule the next call.
